title: Sizing paper for calligraphy
subtitle: Gelatin sizing for hand-made sheets
excerpt: An unsized hand-made paper absorbs calligraphy ink immediately, spreading it through the fibre network and making crisp strokes impossible. Gelatin sizing closes the surface, forcing the ink to dry on top of the fibres rather than inside them. This technique applies a dilute gelatin size to finished dry hand-made sheets.
sourceNotes: Gelatin sizing for calligraphy paper documented in Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), appendix on paper preparation. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on sizing methods. The gelatin-plus-alum sizing formula is the historical European standard.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface treatment applied to paper to reduce its absorbency. Sized paper holds ink on the surface; unsized (waterleaf) paper absorbs ink immediately.
An unsized paper sheet that absorbs water rapidly. Hand-made sheets are waterleaf until sized.
Potassium aluminium sulphate added to gelatin sizing. The alum hardens the gelatin so it does not redissolve on humid days; it also mordants the paper for marbling.

content.text: , they absorb ink as quickly as blotting paper. Gelatin
coats the fibre surfaces and partially closes the inter-fibre spaces, holding ink on the surface long enough for a clean, sharp calligraphy stroke. The
hardens the gelatin and prevents it from re-wetting in humid conditions. The same alum-gelatin sizing also mordants the paper for marbling if a colourful surface is needed.

attrs.title: Alum handling
attrs.body: Alum is a mild skin and eye irritant; wear gloves when mixing the solution and rinse any skin contact promptly.

items.name: Sheet gelatin (food-grade, platinum or gold strength), or powdered gelatin
Potassium aluminium sulphate (alum mordant)
items.qty: 2 g (half the weight of the gelatin)

items.name: Shallow tray, larger than the sheets
Wooden dowel or clean rod for lifting sheets
Clothes line and pegs for drying

content.text: Soak the gelatin sheets in cold water for five minutes until softened. Dissolve the softened gelatin in 500 ml of warm water at approximately 45°C, hot enough to dissolve completely but not boiling. Stir well.
Dissolve the alum separately in 50 ml of warm water, then stir into the gelatin solution. The combined solution should be clear and warm.

Pour the sizing solution into the flat tray to a depth of approximately 30 mm. Wearing gloves, submerge one sheet at a time, pressing it gently down so the solution contacts the entire face. Hold it under for fifteen seconds.
Lift the sheet with the dowel and hang to dry. The gelatin will be visible as a slight sheen on the dry surface; this is normal and does not affect the writing surface.
Once dry, press the sized sheets under heavy books for thirty minutes to flatten them. A wet sheet can distort slightly as it dries.

items.symptom: The sized sheet is still absorbent, ink spreads
items.cause: The gelatin concentration is too low, or the sheet was not submerged long enough.
items.fix: Increase the gelatin to water ratio: try 10 g of gelatin per 500 ml for a more strongly sized sheet. Or dip the sheet twice, drying completely between dips.

items.symptom: The sheet wrinkles badly after sizing
items.cause: The hand-made sheet is not evenly formed, so different areas absorb the sizing solution unevenly and expand at different rates.
items.fix: Press the sized sheet between smooth boards before it is fully dry. Remove from the boards when dry, press boards may cause blocking if left too long.
